chapter_summary: At dawn after the night had passed, the sisters Kadru and Vinata — furious and agitated, bound by a wager that had set slavery as its stake — went together to look closely at the horse Uccaihśravas. On the way they came upon the great ocean, and the chapter unfolds as an extended hymn-like description of it. The sea is the receptacle of all jewels and the abode of Varuna, the lovely dwelling of the Nagas, the best lord of rivers, the seat of the submarine fire of the netherworld and the prison-bond of the Asuras, terrifying to all creatures, holy and divine, the supreme source of amrita — immeasurable, unfathomable, of auspicious waters. Its tossing, dancing waves rise and fall with the waxing and waning of the moon; it is the birthplace of the conch Pancajanya. It is the water that the Lord Govinda, of boundless splendor, stirred and churned when, in the form of a boar, he recovered the earth; whose fathomless netherworld the brahmarshi Atri reached by performing tapas for a hundred years; the yuga-ending couch where Vishnu Padmanabha of immeasurable energy lies in his adhyatma yoga-slumber; the giver of oblation-water to the blazing fire of the mare's mouth. Vast, shoreless, immeasurable, rivaled in vain by thousands of mighty rivers that rush ceaselessly toward it — such was the boundless deep that the two women, gazing on it, swiftly approached.

story.title: The Sisters Behold the Great Ocean
story.arc: Driven by their bitter wager over the color of Uccaihshravas's tail — the loser to become the other's slave — Kadru and Vinata set out at daybreak to inspect the horse at close range. Before they reach it, the narrative pauses on the boundless sea they must pass, rendering it not as mere scenery but as a cosmic site: treasury of jewels, throne of Varuna, lair of serpents, jail of demons, womb of nectar and of the conch Pancajanya, scene of Vishnu's boar-rescue of the earth and of his yuga-ending sleep. The chapter ends with the two women gazing upon this immeasurable deep and hastening on toward it.
story.timeline_note: Takes place the morning after the wager between the sisters; set in the deep mythic past during the era of Kadru and Vinata, framed by Suta's narration to the assembled sages.
story.comic_potential: Two feuding sisters storm off at dawn to settle a bet about a horse's tail — and the poet, in no hurry, lavishes seventeen verses on describing the ocean in the way before letting them get anywhere near the horse.

teachings.teaching: The ocean is depicted as the supreme source and treasury of all that is precious — the receptacle of every jewel and the very mine of amrita, the nectar of immortality — yet it is at once auspicious and divine and a thing of terror to all beings.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya framework the unfathomable sea reads as the boundless inner ocean of consciousness: source of the deathless nectar (amrita) the yogi seeks within, simultaneously serene and overwhelming to the unprepared mind.

teachings.teaching: At the dissolution and renewal of the yuga, Vishnu Padmanabha lies upon the ocean absorbed in adhyatma yoga-nidra, the deep yogic sleep, his couch the very waters of the cosmic deep.
teachings.lens_note: Yoga-nidra here marks the turiya-like state beyond waking and dream that Sharma's Kriya path cultivates — the Lord's withdrawal into the self at the close of a cycle mirrors the practitioner's conscious entry into the silent ground between worlds.

teachings.teaching: The Lord Govinda of boundless splendor, taking the form of a boar, churned and stirred the cosmic waters to recover the earth — the divine descends into the formless deep to restore order to the world.
teachings.lens_note: The boar-descent into the waters echoes the inward plunge of Kriya: consciousness must enter its own depths to lift the grounded earth (the embodied self) back into stability.

teachings.teaching: The brahmarshi Atri reached the unfathomable, imperishable floor of the netherworld beneath the ocean only by performing tapas for a hundred years — the deepest reaches yield only to sustained austerity.
teachings.lens_note: Atri's hundred years of tapas to touch the bottomless depth is the long, patient sadhana of Kriya: the unmeasured ground of being is fathomed not by force but by enduring, concentrated discipline.
